content:
HTML JavaScript Web Development Apple Svelte 4 min read Just now -- Press enter or click to view image in full size Form validation is one of the most important parts of building web applications. Whether you’re creating a login page, registration form, or contact form, validating user input helps prevent invalid data from reaching your server. In this tutorial, we’ll learn how to build a simple authentication form in SvelteKit using Superforms and Zod. By the end of this guide, you’ll understand how to: • Create a validation schema with Zod • Initialize a Superform on the server • Validate submitted data • Display validation errors • Show success messages after a valid submission Prerequisites Before getting started, make sure you have: • Basic knowledge of SvelteKit • Node.js installed • sveltekit-superforms • zod Creating the Validation Schema The first step is creating a schema inside src/lib/schemas/auth.ts. import { z } from 'zod';export const userAuth = z.object({ email: z.email('Please enter a valid email address'), password: z .string() .min(8, 'Password must be at least 8 characters') });This schema defines the rules for our form. • The email field must contain a valid email address. • The password must contain at least eight characters. Instead of writing validation manually, Zod does it for us. Loading the Form Inside +page.server.ts, we initialize the form. export const load = async () => { const form = await superValidate(zod4(userAuth)); return { form }; };The superValidate() function creates a form object using our Zod schema. Notice that we’re wrapping the schema with zod4(). const form = await superValidate(zod4(userAuth));This adapter allows Superforms to understand Zod v4 schemas. The returned form contains: • Initial values • Validation state • Error messages • Metadata These are automatically sent to the page. Handling Form Submission Next, we create a form action. export const actions = { default: async ({ request }) => { const form = await superValidate(request, zod4(userAuth)); if (!form.valid) { return fail(400, { form }); } return message(form, 'Form posted successfully'); } };When the form is submitted, Superforms automatically reads the incoming request. const form = await superValidate(request, zod4(userAuth));Instead of manually extracting every field, Superforms handles everything for us. Checking Validation The validation result is available through form.valid. if (!form.valid) { return fail(400, { form }); }If validation fails: • the page keeps its current values • validation errors are returned • the correct HTTP status (400) is sent back No page reload is required. Returning a Success Message If validation succeeds, we return a success message. return message(form, 'Form posted successfully');This message becomes available on the client automatically. Connecting the Form on the Client Now let’s move to +page.svelte. We first initialize Superforms. const { form, enhance, message, errors } = superForm(data.form);This gives us several useful stores. form Contains the current values. $form.email $form.password errors Contains validation errors returned from the server. $errors.email $errors.password message Contains the success message returned from the server. $message enhance Enhances the form so submissions happen without a full page refresh.
This gives your form a smooth, modern user experience while still using server-side actions. Binding Input Values Each input is connected to the form store. and Whenever the user types, the form store updates automatically. Showing Validation Errors Displaying errors is simple. {#if $errors.email}

{$errors.email}

{/if}Password errors work exactly the same way. {#if $errors.password}

{$errors.password}

{/if}If validation fails on the server, the errors immediately appear beside the corresponding field. Showing Success Messages After a successful submission, simply display the returned message. {#if $message}

{$message}

{/if}Since the message comes from the server, there’s no need to manage additional client-side state. Why Use Superforms? Superforms removes much of the repetitive work involved in building forms. Some of its advantages include: • Automatic server-side validation • Excellent TypeScript support • Seamless integration with Zod • Progressive enhancement • Automatic error handlin • Easy success messages • Minimal boilerplate Instead of manually parsing FormData, validating inputs, and managing errors, Superforms handles these tasks while keeping your code clean and maintainable. Final Thoughts Superforms and Zod make form handling in SvelteKit both simple and robust. With just a few lines of code, you can define a schema, validate incoming requests, display field-specific errors, and provide users with meaningful feedback. This example only scratches the surface. Once you’re comfortable with the basics, you can explore advanced features like nested forms, file uploads, custom validation, multiple forms on the same page, and integration with databases and authentication systems. If you’re building production-ready SvelteKit applications, Superforms is a tool worth learning.
